parts.text: "Project @junit-quickcheck/ does not compile due to a breaking dependency update. Your goal is to generate a GENERIC, REUSABLE transformation rule - not a one-off patch - that can be applied to ANY Maven project affected by the same breaking change. Plan: 1) Identify the compilation errors in @junit-quickcheck/ and determine which API    (class/method/constructor/signature) changed in the dependency.    The new dependency API specification is available at /workspace/mockito-core-4.1.0-api.md and the Javadoc at /workspace/javadoc-mockito-core-4.1.0/    for reference when identifying replacement types, methods, or fields. 2) Characterize the breaking change abstractly:    - What was the old API pattern? (e.g., Foo.bar(String))    - What is the new API pattern? (e.g., Foo.bar(String, boolean))    - What structural transformation does this require at the call site?    Do NOT assume the fix is specific to this project - any project calling the old API will need the same transformation. 3) Generate a GENERIC Spoon transformation using the template in @/workspace/spoon-base-template/:    - Match the old API pattern structurally, not by project-specific class names.    - Use patterns to traverse all files and apply the fix wherever the old pattern appears.    - Parameterize by fully-qualified type names and method signatures from the dependency, NOT from the client.    - Save the transformation in /workspace/spoon-base-template/src/main/java/github/chains/Main.java.    - Use the Spoon API documentation located in folder /workspace/spoon-api-docs/ for reference. 4) Compile and validate the transformation to ensure it has no errors. 5) Execute the transformation to @junit-quickcheck/ and ensure both compilation errors and test errors are fixed. 6) Ensure generalizability: ensure the generated rule contains no hardcoded project-specific identifiers.    The rule must be applicable to other projects by simply changing the input source directory path."

**Considering import changes**

I’m thinking about changing the import reference to a new type and whether that could create a duplicate import issue. If auto imports are enabled, maybe the printer could handle deduplication, but I’m not entirely sure. One option is to disable auto imports to keep explicit ones. However, if the old import remains, it causes compilation fails, so it needs rewriting. Removing it might lead to complications with fully qualified annotations. I wonder if `CtImport` can be replaced with a new import instead.

**Deciding import methods**

I'm considering whether to call `delete()` or `replace(newImport)` for managing imports. It seems I should check the documentation—maybe through `factory.Code().createImport` or `factory.Type().createReference`? I should search for `createImport` to see what options I have available. There’s a lot to think through regarding how to handle this properly, and I want to make sure I'm choosing the right method. Let’s figure this out!

I found the last issue: the model still contains the stale import, so the rewritten source won’t compile yet. I’m switching the rule to rewrite or delete the matching `CtImport`, which makes the transformation correct for any project with the same moved type.
